coredata.dc:description: The doping effect of carbon nanodots (CNDs) in the PC61BM electron-transport layer on the performance of inverted planar MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) having two different kinds of the hole-transport layer, namely organic PEDOT:PSS and inorganic NiOx, was investigated. The CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite layer was deposited in air at 35% humidity. An average 11% and 12% enhancement of the power conversion efficiency (PCE) was achieved for 1 wt% CNDs doping in the PSCs with PEDOT:PSS and NiOx, respectively. This improvement is attributed to high electron density of CNDs resulting in a triple increase of the electrical conductivity of the PC61BM layer and passivation of the perovskite/PC61BM interface that is reflected by an increase of the open-circuit voltage. In line with this, parallel resistance and fill factor of the PSCs are also improved. Moreover, the energy-resolved electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed additional free-charge carriers in the PC61BM layer generated under illumination that were detected via the polaron states formation in the band gap with positive effect on the short-circuit current. All these factors contribute to the PCE improvement. Stability tests of the PSC with PEDOT:PSS under a continuous 24 hour 1.5 AM illumination showed a five times smaller final PCE decrease for the 1 wt% CNDs doping of the PC61BM layer comparing to the undoped counterpart. The passivation effect of CNDs, namely electron filling the traps formed by the photo-dimerization and photo-oxidation of PC61BM molecules, is responsible for this remarkable improvement of the short-term stability.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image, (b) absorption spectrum (left axis) and photoluminescence (right axis) of CNDs. (c) Sketch of the PSCs doped with CNDs.

(a) Typical J-V curves under illumination and (b) PCE statistics of the PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices. (c) Typical J-V curves under illumination and (d) PCE statistics of the PSCs (NiOx) devices. The PC61BM layer was doped with 0–1.5 wt% CNDs. The statistics was done over 10 devices for each doping concentration except for 1.5 wt% CNDs where 5 devices were used.
(a) The EQE spectra of the PSCs (NiOx) and (b) PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices before and after the PC61BM layer doping with 1 wt% CNDs.
(a) Schematic view of the sandwich structure ITO/PC61BM:CNDs/Ca/Ag and (b) its dark J-V curves with 0–3 wt% CNDs doping of the PC61BM layer.

DOS spectra of the PC61BM layer before and after the doping with 1–3 wt% CNDs measured by the ER-EIS method in dark and under illumination in (a) logarithmic and (b) linear scales. The DOS spectrum in dark shown with gray curve is unaffected by the CNDs doping. Details of the band gap in linear scale with the integration intervals of the positive and negative polaron states (colored areas) for: (c) the reference, 0.5, and 1.0 wt% CNDs doped layers, (d) 1.5 and 3 wt% CNDs doped layers. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Dependence of the total concentration of positive and negative polaron states on CNDs concentration. The lines are a guide to the eye.

Temporal evolution of the photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices with the undoped PC61BM layer and after the doping with 1 wt% CNDs under continuous AM 1.5 illumination in air (∼40% humidity) – (a) PCE, (b) FF, (c) Jsc , (d)Voc .

Average photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs (NiOx) and PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices with the 0–1.5 wt% CNDs doping of the PC61BM layer. The averaging was done over 10 devices for each doping concentration except for 1.5 wt% CNDs where 5 devices were used.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The doping effect of carbon nanodots (CNDs) in the PC61BM electron-transport layer on the performance of inverted planar MAPbI3 perovskite solar cells (PSCs) having two different kinds of the hole-transport layer, namely organic PEDOT:PSS and inorganic NiOx, was investigated. The CH3NH3PbI3 perovskite layer was deposited in air at 35% humidity. An average 11% and 12% enhancement of the power conversion efficiency (PCE) was achieved for 1 wt% CNDs doping in the PSCs with PEDOT:PSS and NiOx, respectively. This improvement is attributed to high electron density of CNDs resulting in a triple increase of the electrical conductivity of the PC61BM layer and passivation of the perovskite/PC61BM interface that is reflected by an increase of the open-circuit voltage. In line with this, parallel resistance and fill factor of the PSCs are also improved. Moreover, the energy-resolved electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed additional free-charge carriers in the PC61BM layer generated under illumination that were detected via the polaron states formation in the band gap with positive effect on the short-circuit current. All these factors contribute to the PCE improvement. Stability tests of the PSC with PEDOT:PSS under a continuous 24 hour 1.5 AM illumination showed a five times smaller final PCE decrease for the 1 wt% CNDs doping of the PC61BM layer comparing to the undoped counterpart. The passivation effect of CNDs, namely electron filling the traps formed by the photo-dimerization and photo-oxidation of PC61BM molecules, is responsible for this remarkable improvement of the short-term stability.

ce:section.ce:para: The hybrid organic-inorganic perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have attracted much attention due to their impressive progress in the power conversion efficiency (PCE) exceeding at present 22% (Akihiro et al., 2009; Luo et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2015; W.S. Yang et al., 2017). This high value of PCE of PSCs is mainly attributed to a strong optical absorption, low exciton binding energy, long free carrier diffusion length and unique defect tolerance of the organic-inorganic hybrid perovskite materials. Because of the possibility to apply low-cost solution-based fabrication process, the PSCs are considered one of the most promising candidates for the next-generation photovoltaic devices.

The key parts of the PSCs are the active perovskite layer converting light into electric charges and the electron and hole transport layers that transfer the respective charge carriers to the electrodes. The high quality of the perovskite layer, especially its morphology and crystallinity, is a crucial factor to efficiently transport the photocarriers to the selective contacts. However, the PCE of PSCs depends strongly also on a proper design of the electron and hole transport layers (ETL, HTL). The selective contacts play a fundamental role also in respect to the stability of perovskite solar cells. In fact, the degradation mechanisms are influenced by the perovskite surface cations and surface termination groups. Therefore the surface/interface chemistry of PSCs plays an important role (Chen et al., 2017; Koocher et al., 2015; Li et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016).

Most of the record performance PSCs utilize TiO2 as ETL (Zhou et al., 2018), the highest PCE 22.1% being reported for the devices with mesoporous TiO2 layer (W.S. Yang et al., 2017). Further improvement of the electronic transport properties of the TiO2 layer by doping with Ru (Xu et al., 2018), Nb (G. Yin et al., 2017) was reported. Besides TiO2 (pure or doped), several inorganic materials such as SnO2 (Son et al., 2014), ZnO (Lee et al., 2012), and ZrO2 (Bi et al., 2013) were shown to be good electron transport layer. SnO2, in particular, is employed in the most efficient planar device. The planar configuration is attractive due to the low-temperature processing which is cost effective and compatible with flexible substrates. However, low stability and hysteresis are the main problems still to be solved. Addressing the charge accumulation and recombination at the ETL/perovskite interface is one of the most promising strategies.

The PSCs in the inverted architecture consist of a conductive glass substrate, hole transport layer (HTL), perovskite layer, electron transport layer (ETL) and metallic electrode in this order. The p-i-n structure of perovskite solar cells has shown efficiencies as high as 21% (Luo et al., 2018) with solution-processed secondary growth (SSG) of perovskite layer on poly[bis(4-phenyl)(2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)aminepoly(triarylamine) (PTAA). Heumueller et al. (2016) reported the devices having PCE 18.1% with solubility controlled MAPbl3 deposited on poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)/poly (styrenesulfonic acid)) PEDOT:PSS. The devices with PEDOT:PSS as HTL have low efficiency compared to the devices with PTAA as HTL. However, PEDOT:PSS is a common choice due to its good conductivity, suitable energy level alignment with CH3NH3PbI3, air processability, solution processability and low cost compared to other available HTL materials for the inverted PSCs. Further, various inorganic materials like CuOx (Sun et al., 2016), NiOx (Kwon et al., 2016; Tang et al., 2018; X. Yin et al., 2017) and CuSCN (Xi et al., 2017; Ye et al., 2015) have been utilized as HTL. Among them, NiOx is the most promising due to its suitable energy levels for efficient hole transport and electron blocking as well as good chemical and thermal stability. We have used PEDOT:PSS and NiOx as HTL for our different PSCs.

In the inverted planar PSCs, fullerene C60 (Liu et al., 2016) and fullerene derivatives such as [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) (Jeng et al., 2013) (PCE 3.9%), PCBM/PCBDAN (Xie et al., 2016) (PCE 17.2%), ICBA (Wang et al., 2014) (PCE 12.2%), ICTA (Bi et al., 2014) (PCE 12.5%) and PC71BM (Chiang et al., 2014) (PCE 16.3%) are the mostly used ETL materials. PCBM, in particular, is broadly employed due to its efficient charge separation (Liu et al., 2016), easy and low-temperature processing. Hydrophobicity and good stability under UV light are other benefits of PCBM (Wang et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2018). It has been shown that applying the PCBM/C60 ETL (Shao et al., 2014), the charge trap states at the perovskite/ETL interface are passivated. This has been connected with the mitigation of the hysteresis. The importance of addressing and minimizing the losses located at the perovskite/ETL interface has been exemplified also recently on C60 ETL (Caprioglio et al., 2019).

Another attractive feature of fullerenes is the chance to improve their electron transport properties through doping. In fact, an important increase of the PCBM conductivity was reported for PCBM doped by rGO (Kakavelakis et al., 2017), graphydine (Kuang et al., 2015) and DMBI (Kim et al., 2015). Significant increase of the electrical conductivity and electron mobility has been recently reported by Z. Yang et al. (2017) for PCBM doped with GQDs. From the reported data one can conclude that PCBM doping with carbon or graphene low-dimensional materials is an appealing route to enhance PSCs performance.

In this work, the effect of the PC61BM layer doping with carbon nanodots (CNDs) up to 3 wt% on the performance of inverted planar PSCs is studied. Two types of PSCs with different HTL were fabricated, namely ITO/NiOx/MAPbI3/PC61BM/BCP/Ag and ITO/PEDOT:PSS/MAPbI3/PC61BM/BCP/Ag. The J-V characteristics, external quantum efficiency (EQE) and electrical conductivity measurements were complemented by the density of states (DOS) mapping by the energy-resolved impedance spectroscopy (ER-EIS) (Nádaždy et al., 2014) of a PC61BM layer with the same doping to elucidate the effect of CNDs on PCE.

An average 11% PCE improvement (maximum 18%) for the PSCs with PEDOT:PSS HTL and 12% improvement (maximum 16%) for the PSCs with NiOx HTL were observed for the PC61BM layer doped with a concentration of 1 wt% CNDs. The PCE improvement is related to an increase of the electrical conductivity of the doped PC61BM layer as observed in dark and to an increase of the open-circuit voltage Voc which suggests passivation effect of CNDs resulting in the suppression of traps at the perovskite/ETL interface. The formation of polaron and exciton states in PC61BM under illumination as confirmed by the ER-EIS analysis suggests the generation of additional free-charge carriers that also contribute to the PCE improvement. The passivation effect of CNDs on the photo-oxidation induced defects as well as suppressed photo-dimerization due to electron filling the traps of PC61BM molecules are responsible for the five times smaller PCE decrease after a continuous 24 hour 1.5 AM illumination for the 1 wt% CNDs doping of the PC61BM layer in the PSC with PEDOT:PSS HTL as compared to the undoped counterpart. This shows substantial improvement of short-term stability.

The inverted planar PSCs with NiOx and PEDOT:PSS HTL were fabricated by all-solution method except for the metallic top contact electrode. The PSCs device ITO/NiOx/MAPbI3/PC61BM/BCP/Ag is marked as PSC(NiOx) and ITO/PEDOT:PSS/MAPbI3/PC61BM/BCP/Ag as PSC(PEDOT:PSS) in the following. The PC61BM layer of 80 nm thickness was doped with 0, 0.5, 1, 1.5 wt% CNDs (see the sketch of the devices doped with CNDs in Fig. 1 c). The samples were prepared in a clean room laboratory with a controlled humidity of 35%. The perovskite layer of 350–375 nm thickness was prepared by spin coating applying the antisolvent processing procedure (Girolamo et al., 2018). The method of CNDs preparation is described elsewhere (Bodik et al., 2018).

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CNDs (Fig. 1a) shows the particles of spherical shape with a diameter of 5 ± 0.95 nm. The absorbance spectrum (Fig. 1b, left axis) indicates the π-π* transition of carbon bonds and δ-π* transition of carbon-oxygen bonds located at 242 nm and 360 nm, respectively (Bodik et al., 2018; Stanković et al., 2018). This absorbance is typical for carbon materials and is consistent with different types of carbon nanoparticles (Wang and Hu, 2014). The CNDs have a strong photoluminescence in the blue region (Fig. 1b, right axis).

The effect of the PC61BM doping with CNDs on the current density–voltage (J-V) characteristics under illumination for both kinds of PSCs is shown in Fig. 2 a, c. The devices with undoped PCBM display a 12.4% average efficiency with NiOx as HTL and 12.1% when employing PEDOT:PSS (Fig. 2b, d). These values are in line with p-i-n perovskite solar cells with CH3NH3PbI3 processed in air (Ciro et al., 2017). In general, devices employing NiOx show higher shunt resistance compared to PEDOT:PSS (14 kΩ for NiOx and 2.3 kΩ for PEDOT:PSS in our reference devices) while FF is similar (66.1% for NiOx and 65.2% for PEDOT:PSS).

The photovoltaic parameters derived from J-V curves are summarized in Table 1 . The maximum improvement of all photovoltaic parameters is obtained for the PC61BM doping with 1 wt% CNDs. For the PSCs(PEDOT:PSS) devices, an increase in Jsc from 20.8 mAcm−2 to 21.9 mAcm−2, Voc from 0.89 V to 0.91 V, and fill factor (FF) from 65.2% to 67.2% were found. The PCE increased by ∼11% on average and by 18% at maximum. For the PSCs(NiOx) devices, an increase in Jsc from 19.8 mAcm−2 to 21.2 mAcm−2, Voc from 0.95 V to 0.97 V, and FF from 66.1% to 67.0% were observed. The PCE increased by ∼12% on average and by 16% at maximum.

The increase in Voc for the 1 wt% CNDs doping points to a passivation effect of the CNDs at the perovskite/PC61BM interface which reduces interface recombination. At the same time, FF is slightly increased which suggests that the electron extraction is improved. The passivation effect comes from the high electron density typical for carbon materials such as CNDs. It has been also shown that n-doping of PC61BM such as that with CNDs may improve surface wettability of PC61BM when being spincoated on the perovskite layer which results in fewer voids and other non-radiative defects at the perovskite/PC61BM interface (Tress et al., 2018; Z. Wang et al., 2017). In fact, the shunt resistance increases in the case of NiOx devices from 14 kΩ to 18 kΩ and remains almost unchanged in the case of PEDOT: PSS devices (2.3 kΩ) with 1 wt% CNDs doping. Another mechanism leading to an improvement of the PSC performance can be connected with mobile ionic species associated with vacancies, ions, or interstitial defects (Eames et al., 2015). The migration of such mobile ions causes band bending due to the electric field screening effect or a change of the local doping with impact on the charge extraction efficiency. The internal electric field due to the work function difference between ETL and HTL drives the migration of the positively charged point defects (e.g. iodide vacancy, methylammonium iodide interstitial) toward the HTL, while the negatively charged point defects (e.g. methylammonium iodide vacancy, iodide interstitial) migrate in the opposite direction in dark (Chen et al., 2019). The accumulation of these charged ions at the perovskite interface with ETL and HTL creates an additional electric field across the perovskite film that is opposite to the internal electric field and decreases the photo-generated charge collection. The high electron density of CNDs in the PCBM can act beneficially against such an ion migration and contributes to the enhanced solar cell parameters and improved temporal stability (see below).

The EQE spectra of both types of the PSCs with the undoped PC61BM layer and with the 1 wt% CNDs doping are shown in Fig. 3 . The incorporation of CNDs into PC61BM results in an almost uniform increase of EQE in the entire spectrum ranging from 350 nm to 700 nm. The EQE profiles are similar for both kinds of the PSCs with a maximum EQE of ∼80% without and of ∼90% with the 1 wt% CNDs doping. The UV–VIS–NIR spectroscopy shows that a 350 nm thick perovskite layer totally absorbs the light below the wavelength of 500 nm. It explains the observed uniform increase of the EQE that is not directly related to an increase in the absorbance of the PCBM layer. Rather, it is connected with enhanced drain of free carriers by PCBM layer generated either in the perovskite or PCBM layers. The calculated Jsc values of the PSCs (NiOx) devices without and with the 1 wt% CNDs doping are 19.2 mAcm−2 and 20.7 mAcm−2, respectively, being consistent within 3% with the measured values 19.8 mAcm−2 and 21.2 mA·cm−2. In the case of the PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices without and with the 1 wt% CNDs doping, the calculated Jsc values are 19.9 mAcm−2 and 20.9 mAcm−2, respectively, being consistent within 5% with the measured values 20.8 mAcm−2 and 21.9 mAcm−2. Hence, the Jsc enhancement due to the PC61BM doping with CNDs was confirmed by the integration of the respective EQE spectra. Note that the current gain sets in for both PSCs devices with the 1 wt% CNDs doping above the wavelength of 500 nm (Fig. 3), for which only the incident light through the perovskite layer can be absorbed by the PCBM layer. Therefore, the whole enhancement of the total J SC can be attributed to the additional carrier generation in the 80 nm thick PCBM layer.

To better understand the effect of CNDs on the electron transport in PC61BM, we measured vertically the electrical conductivity of a sandwich structure ITO/PC61BM/Ca/Ag (Fig. 4 a) for 0, 0.5, 1, and 3 wt% CNDs concentrations. An effective area of 0.112 cm2was given by the area of the upper Ca/Ag contact. The dark J-V characteristics are presented in Fig. 4b. The DC conductivity σ0 was determined using the equation (Z. Yang et al., 2017)

where I is the total current, A is the active area (11.2 mm2) and d is the PC61BM layer thickness (80 nm).

A maximum triple increase of the electrical conductivity of the PC61BM layer from 0.003 mScm−1 to 0.009 mScm−1 was found for 1 wt% CNDs. It is attributed to high electron density of CNDs typical for carbon materials which improves also Jsc (Table 1). The decrease in conductivity with excess concentration of CNDs might be attributed to an inhomogeneous mixing of the dopant and PC61BM molecules, in line with a previous report (Z. Wang et al., 2017).

The mechanism of PC61BM doping with CNDs was also studied by the ER-EIS method. The ER-EIS method has been demonstrated as a unique tool for the determination of electronic structure in the entire band gap between HOMO and LUMO bands of organic semiconductors (Nádaždy et al., 2014, 2018; Schauer et al., 2018a, 2018b). Photon absorption in organic semiconductors at individual molecules produces an exciton, a bound state of an electron and a hole. The excitons move by thermal diffusion along the molecular chain segments and de-excite on defects or weaker bonds that may be even disrupted. The opposite process is exciton dissociation on potential jumps into free holes and electrons with subsequent electric polarization of their surroundings called polarons formation (Cobet et al., 2018). Being inherently coupled with the respective free-charge carriers (holes or electrons), the polaron states may serve for their detection which was utilized in our work.

The DOS band gap (g(E)) mapping of a PC61BM layer without and with different CNDs doping was performed at first in dark (Fig. 5 a, b). The g(E) is inversely proportional to the measured charge transfer resistance, R ct (see Experimental section 4). The series resistivity of the electrochemical microcells measured at 1 MHz before each scan was always below 200 Ohm. Therefore, it did not limit the measured R ct varied between 103and 107 Ohm. The g(E) scale was calibrated by assuming the maximum of the HOMO branch to be of the order of ∼1021 cm−3 eV−1 (Vukmirovi and Wang, 2011), which results in the value of 1 × 10−23 cm4 s−1 for the charge-transfer rate constant, k ET. No visible changes due to added CNDs were observed. However, three groups of new states in the band gap appeared under illumination with 532 nm laser, namely close to HOMO and LUMO bands and in the center of the band gap (Fig. 5a, b). The separation of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs is expected to lead to the formation of a pair of polaron states energetically located in the band gap near HOMO and LUMO bands (Cobet et al., 2018). Indeed, such a pair of positive and negative polaron states with a concentration dependent on the CNDs doping can be recognized in Fig. 5a–d. The states in the centre of the band gap are probably connected with the photo-induced charge transfer between the ITO and PCBM (Hemant et al., 2013). The polaron states in the band gap reflect the generation of additional free-charge carriers in the PC61BM layer under illumination. To quantify the changes of the polaron states concentration with the CNDs doping, we integrated the positive and negative polaron states in the energy intervals marked in Fig. 5c, d. The highest concentration of both positive and negative polaron states was found for the 1 wt% CNDs concentration (Fig. 6 ) suggesting generation of the most additional free-charge carriers. The non-monotonous dependence of the polaron concentration on the CNDs concentration can be understood by an interplay between two mechanisms mediated by the CNDs. One mechanism is the above mentioned separation of the photo-excited electron-hole pairs. The second mechanism is the recombination of the already separated charge carriers, which starts prevailing for the CNDs concentration above 1 wt%. Hence, we can conclude two facts. First, in addition to being passive ETL just to mediate the electron transport, even the undoped PC61BM layer actively contributes to the photo-generation of free-charge carriers. Second, the efficiency of the additional free-charge generation in PC61BM can be promoted by the CNDs doping with an optimum at 1 wt%. This effect increases Jsc and also contributes to the PCE improvement by the CNDs doping besides the mechanisms discussed above. A similar effect has also been reported recently by Sidhik et al. on mesoscopicTiO2:CNDs composite as ETL perovskite PSCs (Sidhik et al., 2019).

Stability of PSCs is a crucial problem for their application. It is known that there are several sources of PSCs instability such as moisture, air oxygen and UV light (Bella et al., 2016; Niu et al., 2014). It has been shown that incorporation of graphene and related materials in the structure of PSCs can improve not only the PSCs performance but also their stability (Bi et al., 2017; Y. Wang et al., 2017). Therefore we also studied the effect of CNDs doping on the stability of PSCs (PEDOT:PSS) devices without encapsulation under continuous AM 1.5 illumination in air at ∼40% humidity. The evolution of the photovoltaic parameters of the devices with the undoped PC61BM layer and after the 1 wt% CNDs doping are shown in Fig. 7 . After 24 hour continuous illumination, the PCE of the PSCs with the undoped and doped PC61BM layer dropped by 50.6% and 11.1%, respectively (Fig. 7a). The temporal evolution of the FF, Jsc , and Voc parameters is shown in Fig. 7b–d. In the case of the undoped PCBM, all photovoltaic parameters are steadily decreasing during the period of investigation. In particular, the FF and Jsc are severely reduced, suggesting a deterioration of the ETL that is the most sensitive layer to moisture and oxygen. The FF drop reflects the worsening of parallel and series resistances. The Jsc decrease could be attributed to PC61BM oxidation that lowers DC conductivity (Ka et al., 2018). The smaller PCE decrease of the PSC with the doped PC61BM layer is due mainly to FF while the loss in Voc is smaller than in the undoped case and the Jsc is remarkably stable.

From our results it is evident that the doping of the PC61BM layer with 1 wt% CNDs results in a significant short-term stability increase of the PSCs(PEDOT:PSS) device. There are several factors which could explain this improvement. The photo-dimerization of PC61BM molecules under light exposure resulting in the formation of shallow traps and a decrease of the charge carriers mobility was reported (Distler et al., 2013). It was also shown that photo-dimerization in fullerenes is directly linked to Jsc losses in organic solar cells (Heumueller et al., 2016). In this regard, it is worth noticing that Jsc exhibits the biggest relative decrease in the PSC with the undoped PC61BM layer comparing to FF and Voc (Fig. 7c). This suggests that photo-dimerization of PC61BM molecules is one of the main factors responsible for the PCE degradation observed in the undoped PSC (Heumueller et al., 2016; Mateker and McGehee, 2017). The high electron density of CNDs in the doped device could slow down this process by electron filling the traps induced by photo-dimerization. The photo-oxidation of PC61BM is another important mechanism affecting stability of the solar cells containing PC61BM. The photo-oxidation (Ka et al., 2018) results in shallow electron traps that are nested several tenths of eV into the band gap. The passivation of these defects by CNDs may also contribute to the suppression of the non-radiative recombination and PCE stability. In addition to photo-dimerization and photo-oxidation, the PCE degradation due to nanomorphological disorder induced specifically by UV light was reported (Liu et al., 2017). Although in our case the UV light component is fully absorbed in the perovskite layer (see above), CNDs may act also as morphological stabilizers preventing the PCE degradation. Finally, stability improvement of the PSCs with PC61BM ETL by n-doping has been recently attributed to passivation of the aging-induced defects at the perovskite/PC61BM interface (Z. Wang et al., 2017; Z. Yang et al., 2017) which could be another reason for the stability improvement reported here, being manifested by Voc increase.

In summary, we investigated the effect of CNDs doping of the PC61BM ETL layer in MAPbI3 inverted planar perovskite solar cells. An average 11% and 12% PCE enhancement was achieved for the PSC(PEDOT:PSS) and PSC(NiOx) devices, respectively, when employing 1 wt% CNDs concentration in PCBM. The high electron density of CNDs increases three times the electrical conductivity of the doped PC61BM layer, which is beneficial for the electron collection. Simultaneously, the Voc increase point to a reduction in surface recombination at the perovskite/ETL interface. Moreover, additional free-charge carriers detected by the ER-EIS method via the polaron states in the band gap are generated in the PC61BM layer under illumination with positive effect on Jsc . Such an active role of the PC61BM layer in the PCE improvement has not yet been addressed.

For practical applications, stability issue may be even more important than the absolute PCE value. Stability studies of the PSC (PEDOT:PSS) device under a continuous 24 hour 1.5 AM illumination revealed a nearly five times smaller final PCE decrease for the 1 wt% CNDs doping comparing to the undoped counterpart. The favorable effect of CNDs might come from filling the traps formed by the photo-dimerization and photo-oxidation of PC61BM molecules. The passivation of the traps at the perovskite/ETL interface by CNDs, which is manifested by Voc increase, is another favorable factor contributing to the remarkably improved short-term stability of the PSC.

Dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99%), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), chlorobenzene (CB, 99.8%), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99%), acetone (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. CH3NH3I (MAI, 99.5%) was purchased from Dyesol lmt Sweden, PbI2 (99.99%) was purchased from TCI chemicals. PEDOT:PSS (99.9%), PC61BM (99.9%), bathocuproine (99.9%) were purchased from Ossilla.

Synthesis of carbon nanodots (CNDs): The CNDs solution was prepared by pyrolysis of polyoxyethylene-polyoxypropylene-polyoxyethylene triblock copolymer (Pluronic F-68) applying bottom-up condensation method process used by Mitra et al. (2012) and Bodik et al. (2018).

Preparation of Lead Halide Perovskite Precursor Solutions: Preparation of the precursor solution was the same for both kinds of devices. 225.056 mg MAI and 652.51 mg of PbI2 (1:1) were dissolved in a mixture of 900 µl anhydrous DMF and 100 µl anhydrous DMSO (9:1). The solution was stirred overnight and filtered with 0.45 µl PTFE filter before use.

Device fabrication: The PSCs were fabricated on a pre-patterned 19.5 × 18.2 mm ITO coated glass substrates with a sheet resistance of ≈7 Ωsq−1. The ITO glass was cleaned by a four-step cleaning process (detergent, deionized water, acetone, isopropanol). Before the deposition of HTL layer, the substrates were placed inside plasma for 10 min in order to remove the organic contamination and increase the surface hydrophilicity of the substrates. The PEDOT:PSS was spincoated on the ITO substrate at 5000 rpm for 45 s and the films were annealed at 120 °C for 30 min under ambient conditions. The average thickness of the layer was 40 nm. For NiOx deposition, a nickel acetylacetonate (Alfa Aesar) was dissolved in 2-methoxyethanol with the addition of 20 µl/ml of HNO3 as stabilizer up to a 0.2 M concentration (Girolamo et al., 2018).

The solution was stirred overnight at room temperature and then spincoated on ITO at 4000 rpm for 30 s, followed by an annealing at 300 °C for 1 h. After the substrates were cooled down to room temperature, the perovskite solution was spincoated in air with humidity 35% on HTL covered substrates at 1000 rpm for 15 s and 5000 rpm for another 45 s. About 5 s after the initiation of the second spin coating step, diethyl ether/chlorobenzene (0.6 ml) was dropped onto the substrates. The films were annealed at 50 °C (in dark) for 2 min and at 100 °C for 10 min on a hot plate (thickness 375 nm). The substrates were again cooled down to room temperature. Different concentrations of CNDs (0%, 0.5%, 1%, and 1.5%) were added to the PC61BM having an initial concentration of 20 mg ml−1 in chlorobenzene and stirred for at least 5 h. Afterwards different ETL solutions were spin coated on the top of perovskite films at 1000 rpm for 30 s in a nitrogen-filled glove box (thickness ∼80 nm for all layers). After 10 min, 1.5 mg BCP in 1 ml ethanol was spincoated on the top of the ETL at 4000 rpm for 30 s. Finally, 125 nm of silver was deposited through a shadow mask by thermal evaporation. For all devices, the active area was 11.2 mm2. The air humidity in the laboratory was ∼40%. The PC61BM layer for the ER-EIS measurements was prepared in the same way as the ETL layer of PSCs. Different concentrations of CNDs (0%, 0.5%, 1%, and 1.5%) were added to the PC61BM having an initial concentration of 20 mg ml−1 in chlorobenzene and stirred for at least 5 h. Afterwards different solutions were spin coated on the top of the ITO substrates with a sheet resistance of ≈7 Ωsq−1 at 1000 rpm for 30 s in a nitrogen-filled glove box (thickness ∼80 nm for all layers).

Characterization and measurements: The prepared CNDs solutions were dropped and dried on standard TEM grids with ultrathin amorphous carbon films and inspected by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (ARM200CF, Jeol). The UV–VIS–NIR absorbance was measured by the Solidspec-3700 (SHIMAZDU, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) of CNDs was measured using spectrofluorometer Fluorolog-3 (Horiba, France) at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm. The ER-EIS method measures the DOS in organic semiconductor as a function of the energy versus vacuum (Nádaždy et al., 2014; Schauer et al., 2018a, 2018b) Unlike cyclic voltammetry where the current measured during linear voltage sweeping comprises not only faradaic component, the ER-EIS signal is measured at a frequency where the charge transfer resistance of redox reactions at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface determines the real part of the impedance. Hence, the charge transfer resistance is experimentally obtained as the real part of complex impedance. The desired DOS function of electronic transport, g(E), can be derived directly from the measured charge transfer resistance of a semiconductor/electrolyte interface, R CT, at the position of the Fermi energy, E F (Schauer et al., 2018a, 2018b).

where e is the elementary charge, k ET the charge-transfer rate constant (assumed to be energy-independent), S the sample area, and [A] the concentration of the electrolyte redox species in the interphase region of the solid-liquid contact. One of the advantages of the ER-EIS method is that there is no need to perform complete EIS spectra and it is sufficient to measure impedance at a given frequency, as it has been shown in our recent paper (Schauer et al., 2018a, 2018b). The ER-EIS measurements were performed in a glove box with protective N2 atmosphere with oxygen and moisture below 20 ppm and 2 ppm, respectively. A common three-electrode electrochemical microcell with a volume of ∼200 μl and working ITO electrode area of ∼12 mm2 was exploited. Solution of 0.1 M TBAPF6 in acetonitrile was used as the supporting electrolyte. The potential of the working electrode with respect to the reference Ag/AgCl electrode was controlled via a home-built potentiostat. Pt wire was used as the counter electrode. The energy recorded relative to the reference Ag/AgCl electrode can be referred to the local vacuum level given that the Ag/AgCl energy differs from the vacuum value by 4.66 eV. The impedance/gain-phase analyzer Solartron analytical, model 1260, was used with a frequency set to 0.5 Hz, an amplitude of AC voltage set to 100 mV, and a sweep rate of the DC voltage ramp set to 10 mV/s (Nádaždy et al., 2014). The photo-induced states were generated by green laser illumination at 532 nm during the measurement. The presence of polaron states in the DOS band gap was studied by the changes between the DOS measured in dark and under the illumination. The performance of the devices was measured under ambient atmosphere with an Air Mass 1.5 Global (A.M. 1.5 G) solar simulator at an intensity of 100 mWcm−2 using a class A sun simulator (ABET). A reference monocrystalline silicon solar cell from Newport Corp. was used to calibrate the light intensity. For the degradation study, the as-fabricated cells were exposed to continuous solar irradiation without encapsulation, using the A.M. 1.5 G solar simulator under ambient conditions with room humidity permanently above 45%. External quantum efficiency (EQE) values were measured using SpeQuest system with a power source 150 W Xe Arc lamp, LSN 153 and monochromator Omni-λ300 Grating.
